subject: ap-precalculus
title: Sinusoidal Modeling: Seasonal Temperature Variation
stimulus: The average daily high temperature in a city varies periodically throughout the year. The highest average temperature of $95^\circ$F occurs on day $201$ (July 20) and the lowest average temperature of $35^\circ$F occurs on day $20$ (January 20). Let $t$ represent the day of the year, where $t = 1$ is January 1. The sinusoidal function $W$ models the average daily high temperature, in degrees Fahrenheit, as a function of $t$.

parts.prompt:
The function $W$ can be written in the form $W(t) = a\sin(b(t - c)) + d$.

(i) Find the values of constants $a$, $b$, $c$, and $d$.

(ii) Using the function $W$, find the average temperature predicted on day $t = 110$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The key insight is that in $f(t) = a\sin(b(t-c)) + d$, the maximum occurs when the argument $b(t-c) = \pi/2$, i.e., at $t = c + \frac{1}{4} \cdot \text{period}$. For max at $t = 201$: $201 = c + 365/4$, so $c = 201 - 91.25 \approx 109.75 \approx 110$. The cosine form $-30\cos(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-20)) + 65$ is simpler: cosine reaches minimum ($-1$) at its argument $= 0$, so minimum at $t = 20$ and maximum one half-period later at $t = 20 + 182.5 = 202.5 \approx 201$. Award full credit for either equivalent form. For part (ii), $t = 110$ is the midline-ascending crossing, giving $W = 65°F$ exactly (with the $c = 110$ parameterization).

scoring_points.description: Correct parameter values — amplitude $a = 30$ (from $\frac{95 - 35}{2} = 30$), midline $d = 65$ (from $\frac{95 + 35}{2} = 65$), period $\approx 365$ days (from minimum at $t = 20$ to maximum at $t = 201$, a span of $181$ days $=$ half period, so period $= 362$ days; accept period $= 365$ for simplicity), $b = \frac{2\pi}{365}$ (accept $\frac{2\pi}{362}$); the sine reaches maximum at $t = c$, so $c = 201$ when using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 201)\right) + 65$... but this places the maximum at $t = 201 + \frac{365}{4} \approx 292$, not at $t = 201$; the correct phase shift for maximum at $t = 201$ is $c \approx 110$ (since the argument reaches $\frac{\pi}{2}$ when $t - c = \frac{365}{4}$, giving $c = 201 - \frac{365}{4} \approx 110$); accept $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$ OR the equivalent cosine form $W(t) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 20)\right) + 65$ (minimum at $t = 20$)

required_elements: Correct parameter values — amplitude $a = 30$ (from $\frac{95 - 35}{2} = 30$), midline $d = 65$ (from $\frac{95 + 35}{2} = 65$), period $\approx 365$ days (from minimum at $t = 20$ to maximum at $t = 201$, a span of $181$ days $=$ half period, so period $= 362$ days; accept period $= 365$ for simplicity), $b = \frac{2\pi}{365}$ (accept $\frac{2\pi}{362}$); the sine reaches maximum at $t = c$, so $c = 201$ when using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 201)\right) + 65$... but this places the maximum at $t = 201 + \frac{365}{4} \approx 292$, not at $t = 201$; the correct phase shift for maximum at $t = 201$ is $c \approx 110$ (since the argument reaches $\frac{\pi}{2}$ when $t - c = \frac{365}{4}$, giving $c = 201 - \frac{365}{4} \approx 110$); accept $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$ OR the equivalent cosine form $W(t) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 20)\right) + 65$ (minimum at $t = 20$)
alternatives.correct_example: Correct parameter values — amplitude $a = 30$ (from $\frac{95 - 35}{2} = 30$), midline $d = 65$ (from $\frac{95 + 35}{2} = 65$), period $\approx 365$ days (from minimum at $t = 20$ to maximum at $t = 201$, a span of $181$ days $=$ half period, so period $= 362$ days; accept period $= 365$ for simplicity), $b = \frac{2\pi}{365}$ (accept $\frac{2\pi}{362}$); the sine reaches maximum at $t = c$, so $c = 201$ when using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 201)\right) + 65$... but this places the maximum at $t = 201 + \frac{365}{4} \approx 292$, not at $t = 201$; the correct phase shift for maximum at $t = 201$ is $c \approx 110$ (since the argument reaches $\frac{\pi}{2}$ when $t - c = \frac{365}{4}$, giving $c = 201 - \frac{365}{4} \approx 110$); accept $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$ OR the equivalent cosine form $W(t) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 20)\right) + 65$ (minimum at $t = 20$)

scoring_points.description: Correct temperature at $t = 110$ — using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$: $W(110) = 30\sin(0) + 65 = 65^\circ$F (the midline); the model predicts an average temperature of $65^\circ$F on day $110$ (approximately April 20); accept any answer consistent with the correctly parameterized model, including exact form

required_elements: Correct temperature at $t = 110$ — using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$: $W(110) = 30\sin(0) + 65 = 65^\circ$F (the midline); the model predicts an average temperature of $65^\circ$F on day $110$ (approximately April 20); accept any answer consistent with the correctly parameterized model, including exact form
alternatives.correct_example: Correct temperature at $t = 110$ — using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$: $W(110) = 30\sin(0) + 65 = 65^\circ$F (the midline); the model predicts an average temperature of $65^\circ$F on day $110$ (approximately April 20); accept any answer consistent with the correctly parameterized model, including exact form

parts.prompt:
(i) Find all days $t$ where $1 \leq t \leq 365$ on which the model predicts an average temperature of exactly $65^\circ$F. Express answers as exact values.

(ii) On the interval $20 < t < 201$ (from the minimum to the maximum temperature), is $W$ increasing or decreasing? Describe the concavity of the graph of $W$ on this interval, noting any changes.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the midline crossings ($W = 65^\circ$F) within one year occur at $t = 110$ (approximately April 20, ascending through midline) and $t = 292.5$ (approximately October 19, descending through midline). The exact value $t = 585/2$ is acceptable. For part (ii), the function increases from its minimum at $t=20$ to its maximum at $t=201$. The inflection point (where concavity changes) is at the midline crossing $t = 110$, which is halfway between the minimum ($t=20$) and maximum ($t=201$) in terms of function behavior. A response that says 'always concave up' or 'always concave down' without noting the change does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Correct days — using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$: setting $W(t) = 65$ gives $\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-110)\right) = 0$, so $\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-110) = k\pi$ for integer $k$; solutions $t = 110 + \frac{365k}{2}$; in $[1, 365]$: $k = 0$ gives $t = 110$ and $k = 1$ gives $t = 110 + 182.5 = 292.5$; exact answers $t = 110$ and $t = \frac{585}{2}$ (accept decimal $292.5$); both required with no incorrect extras

required_elements: Correct days — using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$: setting $W(t) = 65$ gives $\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-110)\right) = 0$, so $\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-110) = k\pi$ for integer $k$; solutions $t = 110 + \frac{365k}{2}$; in $[1, 365]$: $k = 0$ gives $t = 110$ and $k = 1$ gives $t = 110 + 182.5 = 292.5$; exact answers $t = 110$ and $t = \frac{585}{2}$ (accept decimal $292.5$); both required with no incorrect extras
alternatives.correct_example: Correct days — using $W(t) = 30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) + 65$: setting $W(t) = 65$ gives $\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-110)\right) = 0$, so $\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-110) = k\pi$ for integer $k$; solutions $t = 110 + \frac{365k}{2}$; in $[1, 365]$: $k = 0$ gives $t = 110$ and $k = 1$ gives $t = 110 + 182.5 = 292.5$; exact answers $t = 110$ and $t = \frac{585}{2}$ (accept decimal $292.5$); both required with no incorrect extras

scoring_points.description: Correct descriptions — $W$ is increasing throughout $(20, 201)$ (moving from minimum $35^\circ$F to maximum $95^\circ$F); the concavity changes: $W$ is concave up on $(20, 110)$ (rate of increase is increasing) and concave down on $(110, 201)$ (rate of increase is decreasing); the inflection point occurs at $t = 110$ where $W = 65^\circ$F (the midline crossing)

required_elements: Correct descriptions — $W$ is increasing throughout $(20, 201)$ (moving from minimum $35^\circ$F to maximum $95^\circ$F); the concavity changes: $W$ is concave up on $(20, 110)$ (rate of increase is increasing) and concave down on $(110, 201)$ (rate of increase is decreasing); the inflection point occurs at $t = 110$ where $W = 65^\circ$F (the midline crossing)
alternatives.correct_example: Correct descriptions — $W$ is increasing throughout $(20, 201)$ (moving from minimum $35^\circ$F to maximum $95^\circ$F); the concavity changes: $W$ is concave up on $(20, 110)$ (rate of increase is increasing) and concave down on $(110, 201)$ (rate of increase is decreasing); the inflection point occurs at $t = 110$ where $W = 65^\circ$F (the midline crossing)

parts.prompt: A meteorologist claims that the model $W$ is equivalent to $W(t) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - c_0)\right) + 65$ for some value of $c_0$. Find the value of $c_0$ such that this cosine version has its minimum at $t = 20$. Show the trigonometric identity or reasoning used to connect the sine and cosine forms.
parts.scoring_notes: The simplest approach is direct: $-30\cos(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - c_0))$ has its minimum when $\cos = 1$, i.e., when $\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - c_0) = 0$, giving $t = c_0$. For minimum at $t = 20$, $c_0 = 20$. This is the most elegant derivation. Alternatively, using the identity $-\cos\theta = \sin(\theta - \pi/2)$ applied to the sine form connects $c_0 = 18.75 \approx 20$. Accept $c_0 = 20$ if the student directly invokes 'minimum of $-\cos$ occurs at argument $= 0$.' Both the identity and the direct reasoning approaches earn full credit.

scoring_points.description: Correct identity stated and applied — $\sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2}) = -\cos(\theta)$, so $30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110) + \frac{\pi}{2}\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}\left(t - 110 + \frac{365}{4}\right)\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110 + 91.25)\right)$; OR equivalently, $-\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2})$ connecting the forms

required_elements: Correct identity stated and applied — $\sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2}) = -\cos(\theta)$, so $30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110) + \frac{\pi}{2}\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}\left(t - 110 + \frac{365}{4}\right)\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110 + 91.25)\right)$; OR equivalently, $-\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2})$ connecting the forms
alternatives.correct_example: Correct identity stated and applied — $\sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2}) = -\cos(\theta)$, so $30\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110)\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110) + \frac{\pi}{2}\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}\left(t - 110 + \frac{365}{4}\right)\right) = -30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 110 + 91.25)\right)$; OR equivalently, $-\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2})$ connecting the forms

scoring_points.description: Correct value $c_0 = 110 - 91.25 = 18.75 \approx 20$ (accept exact $\frac{75}{4}$ or $18.75$; accept $c_0 = 20$ with reasoning that the minimum of $-30\cos(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 20))$ occurs when $\cos = 1$, i.e., at $t = 20$, which matches the problem's minimum day); the cosine form $-30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 20)\right) + 65$ is the standard answer

required_elements: Correct value $c_0 = 110 - 91.25 = 18.75 \approx 20$ (accept exact $\frac{75}{4}$ or $18.75$; accept $c_0 = 20$ with reasoning that the minimum of $-30\cos(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 20))$ occurs when $\cos = 1$, i.e., at $t = 20$, which matches the problem's minimum day); the cosine form $-30\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 20)\right) + 65$ is the standard answer
